hints.junior: If your table feels slow as rows are added, check the listener count. One listener on the parent handles every row — present, future, and dynamically added.
hints.mid: event.target.closest() is the key — it walks up from the clicked element and finds the row or button you care about, even if the click landed on a nested span.
hints.staff: In vanilla JS: delegation + data attributes (data-action, data-id) on elements is the clean pattern. Avoid checking className or tagName — data attributes are more explicit.

hints.junior: Try adding 200 rows in broken mode — see the slowdown? Now try fixed mode. Same rows, one listener, instant.
hints.mid: Check the Event Listeners panel in DevTools Elements tab. Broken: dozens of entries on rows. Fixed: one entry on the container.
hints.staff: The memory footprint difference is significant at 1000+ rows. Each DOM listener is a retained reference — per-row listeners on removed rows that weren't cleaned up are classic memory leaks.

anecdotes.junior: I once profiled a dashboard that took 2 seconds to render 500 rows — 80% of the time was attaching click listeners. Delegation cut it to 50ms.
anecdotes.mid: React developers sometimes add addEventListener manually per list item, not knowing React already delegates at the root. It adds listeners on top of React's own system.
anecdotes.staff: Event delegation is also the pattern behind 'virtual list' click handling — rows are recycled DOM nodes, and a single delegated listener handles all of them.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're making large tables fast by using one event listener instead of one per row. It's called event delegation, and it's one of the most useful DOM patterns.
sections.mid: This case covers event delegation — how event bubbling makes it work, why closest() is the key primitive, and when not to use it.
sections.staff: We're covering the full delegation toolkit: closest, matches, focusin/focusout for non-bubbling events, and the React synthetic event system interaction.
